content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission,
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660           322-5901              322-6441              322-6444


                                        November 7, 1983


Assemblywoman Gloria Molina
5261 East Beverly Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90022                   Re: FPPC No. A-83-258

Dear Assemblywoman Molina:

     This is in reply to your letter dated October 19, 1983,
regarding funds donated to the Hispanic Caucus leadership
conference to be held in March.  You informed me in our tele-
phone conversation on October 31, that the Caucus is not a
"committee" under the Political Reform Act, and that the
purpose of the Caucus is to inform and interact with members
of the Hispanic community regarding legislative matters.  As
such, the Caucus does not normally solicit or receive funds
from the public.  However, the Caucus is planning to accept
donations from corporations to help defray the costs of its
leadership conference.  You indicated that the Caucus intends
to set up a separate account to handle the receipts and payments
in connection with the conference.  The donations from the
corporations will be used to pay for program materials, rental
of the facilities, and possibly travel for some of the public
participants.  The donated funds will not be used to pay the
expenses of the legislative officials who will attend the
conference.  You are unsure whether any meals will be provided.

     You asked in your letter whether money donated by the
corporations can be used to pay a coordinator to organize the
event.  There is nothing in the Political Reform Act which
would prohibit funds donated for the conference from being
used to pay a coordinator.

     You have also asked whether the Caucus must report the
receipts and payments in connection with the conference.  Such
receipts and payments are not required to be reported under
the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform
Act because the payments are not for "political purposes" as
that term is defined in Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Sections 18215 and 18225.

     However, if the corporations which make payments to the
Caucus are lobbyist employers under Gov. Code Section 86109(a)

Assemblywoman Gloria Molina
November 7, 1983
Page Two


or persons required to file lobbying disclosure reports under
Gov. Code Section 86109(b), the corporations must report the
payments on their quarterly reports (Form 650) filed under the
lobbying disclosure provisions of the Act. We would be glad
to provide specific advice to the lobbyist employers regarding
their reporting obligations.

During our telephone conversation, you suggested that
the Caucus would like to submit a letter to the Fair Political
Practices Commission explaining the purpose of the Caucus leader-
ship conference, who has made payments for the conference, and
how the money was spent by the Caucus. Although there is no
requirement under the Political Reform Act that the Caucus report
to the Commission in this manner, we would be happy to accept
such a letter, and would provide it to anyone who had any questions
about the money received by the Caucus.

Thank you for your inquiry. Please call me if you
have any additional questions.

Sincerely,

Jeanne Pritchard
Political Reform Consultant

SACRAMENTO
STATE CAPITOL 95804
445-1670

DISTRICT OFFICE
5261 EAST BEVERLY BLVD
LOS ANGELES CA 90022
(213) 721-5557

GENEVA VEGA
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

Assembly
California Legislature

COMMITTEES
PUBLIC EMPLOYEES
AND RETIREMENT (VICE CHAIRWOMAN)
LABOR AND EMPLOYMENT
EDUCATION
UTILITIES AND COMMERCE
SELECT COMMITTEE ON
SMALL BUSINESS

COMMISSION OF THE
CALIFORNIA'S

OCT 23 9:40 AM '83

GLORIA MOLINA
ASSEMBLYWOMAN, FIFTY-SIXTH DISTRICT


October 19, 1983


Ms. Jeannie Prichard
Fair Political Practices Commission
100 K Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804


Dear Jeannie,

As chairwoman of the Hispanic Caucus I am organizing a leadership
conference to be held in March. Various corporations have
already expressed interest in the event.

My question is, can the money donated for the conference be used
to pay a coordinator to organize the event? Please respond to
my question as soon as possible.

Sincerely,

Gloria Molina

GLORIA MOLINA

GM/av

sections.question: Can the money donated by corporations for the Hispanic Caucus leadership conference be used to pay a coordinator to organize the event? Must the Caucus report the receipts and payments in connection with the conference?
sections.conclusion: There is nothing in the Political Reform Act which would prohibit funds donated for the conference from being used to pay a coordinator. Such receipts and payments are not required to be reported under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act because the payments are not for 'political purposes.' However, if the corporations making payments are lobbyist employers or persons required to file lobbying disclosure reports, they must report the payments on their quarterly reports (Form 650) under the lobbying disclosure provisions.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether corporate donations to a Hispanic Caucus leadership conference can be used to pay a coordinator and whether such receipts and payments must be reported under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Corporate donations can be used to pay a coordinator without violating the Political Reform Act. Campaign disclosure reporting is not required for conference receipts and payments, but lobbyist employers must report such payments on their lobbying disclosure forms.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (October 19, 1983 letter from Molina) and FPPC response (November 7, 1983 letter from Jeanne Pritchard). Extracted information from the FPPC response as the primary advice letter. Question and conclusion sections clearly identifiable from context despite lack of explicit headers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can the money donated by corporations for the Hispanic Caucus leadership conference be used to pay a coordinator to organize the event? Must the Caucus report the receipts and payments in connection with the conference?

CONCLUSION: There is nothing in the Political Reform Act which would prohibit funds donated for the conference from being used to pay a coordinator. Such receipts and payments are not required to be reported under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act because the payments are not for 'political purposes.' However, if the corporations making payments are lobbyist employers or persons required to file lobbying disclosure reports, they must report the payments on their quarterly reports (Form 650) under the lobbying disclosure provisions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that corporate donations to a Hispanic Caucus leadership conference may be used to pay for a coordinator and that no campaign disclosure reporting is required for such funds, though lobbyist employers must report the payments on their lobbying disclosure reports.
